title: 01 - The Cell as a Unit of Health and Disease - Flashcards

chapter.title: The Cell as a Unit of Health and Disease

description: Third-party generated Chapter 1 flashcards optimized for active recall, spaced repetition, and exam revision. The deck follows the five syllabus buckets and prioritizes high-yield mechanisms, distinctions, and associations from Robbins Basic Pathology, 10th Edition, Chapter 1.

disclaimer.endorsement_notice: These flashcards are third-party generated study materials. They are not produced by, reviewed by, approved by, endorsed by, or affiliated with the textbook authors, Elsevier, the Royal College of Pathologists of Australasia, or any other authority, institution, publisher, or examining body.
disclaimer.use_note: Use these flashcards as revision support alongside the original source material.

deck.deck_name: Pathology::Chapter 01 - The Cell as a Unit of Health and Disease
topic_buckets: Cellular housekeeping, cellular metabolism, and mitochondrial function
Cellular activation, signaling, and growth factors
Maintaining cell populations and regenerative medicine
deck.design_note: Cards are kept mostly single-fact and under one question per card for rapid recall.
deck.page_reference_note: The [n] citations in card backs and the source_page_refs arrays use the printed book page number from Robbins Basic Pathology 10th Edition, not the sequential PDF page position. Chapter 1 occupies book pages 1–29.

cards.bucket: Genome and epigenome
cards.front: What proportion of the human genome encodes proteins?
cards.back: About 1.5%. Only a small fraction of the genome is protein-coding; much disease-related regulation sits in noncoding DNA. [1][2]

cards.bucket: Genome and epigenome
cards.front: What type of DNA element can increase transcription from a distance?
cards.back: An enhancer. Enhancers are noncoding regulatory elements that can increase transcription from a distance. [2]

cards.bucket: Genome and epigenome
cards.front: What term describes large duplicated, deleted, or rearranged DNA segments?
cards.back: Copy number variation (CNV). These are large contiguous genomic gains, losses, or rearrangements. [2][3]

cards.bucket: Genome and epigenome
cards.front: What term describes coinheritance of nearby variants because of physical proximity?
cards.back: Linkage disequilibrium. Nearby variants tend to be inherited together because they sit close together on the chromosome. [2][3]

cards.bucket: Genome and epigenome
cards.front: What does epigenetics refer to?
cards.back: Heritable change in gene expression without an altered DNA sequence. This explains different phenotypes from the same genome. [3]

cards.bucket: Genome and epigenome
cards.front: What is the usual effect of DNA methylation at regulatory regions?
cards.back: Chromatin condensation and transcriptional silencing. DNA methylation typically represses transcription at regulatory regions. [4]

cards.bucket: Genome and epigenome
cards.front: What is the usual effect of histone acetylation?
cards.back: Open chromatin with increased transcription. Histone acetylation generally makes DNA more transcriptionally accessible. [3][4]

cards.bucket: Genome and epigenome
cards.front: What is the main action of microRNAs?
cards.back: Post-transcriptional gene silencing. microRNAs act through RISC to cleave target mRNA or repress translation. [4][5]

cards.bucket: Genome and epigenome
cards.front: What is XIST?
cards.back: A long noncoding RNA that mediates X-chromosome inactivation. [4][5]

cards.bucket: Genome and epigenome
cards.front: Which DNA repair pathway enables precise CRISPR correction when donor DNA is supplied?
cards.back: Homology-directed repair (HDR). This is the precise pathway used when donor DNA is available. [5][6]

cards.bucket: Genome and epigenome
cards.front: Which repair pathway is more error-prone after a double-strand DNA break: NHEJ or HDR?
cards.back: Nonhomologous end joining (NHEJ). It commonly introduces insertions or deletions after double-strand breaks. [5][6]

cards.bucket: Cellular housekeeping, cellular metabolism, and mitochondrial function
cards.front: Which membrane phospholipid flips outward during apoptosis as an "eat me" signal?
cards.back: Phosphatidylserine. It normally sits on the inner leaflet and flips outward during apoptosis. [8]

cards.bucket: Cellular housekeeping, cellular metabolism, and mitochondrial function
cards.front: What is the immediate membrane-transport consequence of ATP depletion?
cards.back: Failure of ion pumps with osmotic cell swelling. ATP depletion prevents the cell from pumping out inorganic ions. [9]

cards.bucket: Cellular housekeeping, cellular metabolism, and mitochondrial function
cards.front: Which process internalizes LDL through specific surface receptors?
cards.back: Clathrin-mediated receptor-mediated endocytosis. LDL uptake is the classic example. [10][11]

cards.bucket: Cellular housekeeping, cellular metabolism, and mitochondrial function
cards.front: What do tight junctions primarily maintain?
cards.back: Paracellular barrier formation and apical-basolateral polarity. [12]

cards.bucket: Cellular housekeeping, cellular metabolism, and mitochondrial function
cards.front: What do gap junctions allow to pass between adjacent cells?
cards.back: Ions and small molecules. Gap junctions help coordinate tissues such as cardiac muscle. [12]

cards.bucket: Cellular housekeeping, cellular metabolism, and mitochondrial function
cards.front: Cytokeratin expression points to what cell lineage?
cards.back: Epithelial lineage. Cytokeratins are epithelial intermediate filaments. [11]

cards.bucket: Cellular housekeeping, cellular metabolism, and mitochondrial function
cards.front: Which organelle synthesizes and folds membrane and secreted proteins?
cards.back: Rough endoplasmic reticulum. It synthesizes and folds membrane and secreted proteins. [12]

cards.bucket: Cellular housekeeping, cellular metabolism, and mitochondrial function
cards.front: Where is the common misfolded CFTR mutant retained?
cards.back: Rough endoplasmic reticulum. The common CFTR mutant is misfolded, retained, and degraded there. [12]

cards.bucket: Cellular housekeeping, cellular metabolism, and mitochondrial function
cards.front: What Golgi-added signal targets enzymes to lysosomes?
cards.back: Mannose-6-phosphate. This Golgi-added tag directs hydrolases to lysosomes. [12][13]

cards.bucket: Cellular housekeeping, cellular metabolism, and mitochondrial function
cards.front: What is the main role of the Golgi apparatus?
cards.back: Protein modification, sorting, and trafficking. The Golgi sends cargo to the membrane, lysosomes, or secretory vesicles. [12][13]

cards.bucket: Cellular housekeeping, cellular metabolism, and mitochondrial function
cards.front: What degrades ubiquitin-tagged cytosolic proteins?
cards.back: Proteasome. It degrades ubiquitin-tagged cytosolic proteins, including short-lived regulatory proteins. [13][14]

cards.bucket: Cellular housekeeping, cellular metabolism, and mitochondrial function
cards.front: What process delivers senescent organelles to lysosomes in a double-membrane vesicle?
cards.back: Autophagy. Senescent organelles are enclosed in an autophagosome before lysosomal degradation. [13][14]

cards.bucket: Cellular housekeeping, cellular metabolism, and mitochondrial function
cards.front: Why is mitochondrial inheritance usually maternal?
cards.back: Because the ovum contributes nearly all cytoplasmic organelles to the zygote. This makes mitochondrial inheritance usually maternal. [13][14]

cards.bucket: Cellular housekeeping, cellular metabolism, and mitochondrial function
cards.front: What metabolic shift defines the Warburg effect?
cards.back: Increased glycolysis with lactate production despite adequate oxygen. This preserves carbon skeletons for biosynthesis. [15]

cards.bucket: Cellular housekeeping, cellular metabolism, and mitochondrial function
cards.front: Release of what mitochondrial protein promotes apoptosis?
cards.back: Cytochrome c. Its release from mitochondria helps trigger apoptosis. [15]

cards.bucket: Cellular housekeeping, cellular metabolism, and mitochondrial function
cards.front: Which second messenger mediates nitric oxide signaling in vascular smooth muscle?
cards.back: cGMP. Nitric oxide activates guanylyl cyclase in vascular smooth muscle. [16]

cards.bucket: Cellular activation, signaling, and growth factors
cards.front: What activates most receptor tyrosine kinases?
cards.back: Ligand-induced dimerization with autophosphorylation. That is the core activation mechanism for receptor tyrosine kinases. [16][17]

cards.bucket: Cellular activation, signaling, and growth factors
cards.front: What is the key signaling event in Notch activation?
cards.back: Proteolytic receptor cleavage with nuclear translocation of the intracellular fragment. That is the hallmark of Notch signaling. [17][18]

cards.bucket: Cellular activation, signaling, and growth factors
cards.front: What molecule is stabilized in Wnt/Frizzled signaling?
cards.back: Beta-catenin. Wnt signaling stabilizes beta-catenin and allows nuclear signaling. [18]

cards.bucket: Cellular activation, signaling, and growth factors
cards.front: What is the effect of delayed GTP hydrolysis in mutant RAS?
cards.back: Persistent proliferative signaling. Delayed GTP hydrolysis keeps RAS in its active state. [17]

cards.bucket: Cellular activation, signaling, and growth factors
cards.front: Which growth factor is the major driver of angiogenesis?
cards.back: VEGF. It is the major driver of angiogenesis. [20]

cards.bucket: Cellular activation, signaling, and growth factors
cards.front: Which growth factor also increases vascular permeability during vessel growth?
cards.back: VEGF. It increases vascular permeability as well as angiogenesis. [20]

cards.bucket: Cellular activation, signaling, and growth factors
cards.front: Which cytokine or growth factor drives ECM synthesis and fibrosis through Smad signaling?
cards.back: TGF-beta. It signals through Smads to promote ECM synthesis and fibrosis. [20][21]

cards.bucket: Cellular activation, signaling, and growth factors
cards.front: Which receptor class is used by steroid hormones?
cards.back: Intracellular or nuclear receptors. Steroid hormones cross the membrane and regulate transcription directly. [16]

cards.bucket: Cellular activation, signaling, and growth factors
cards.front: What is another name for hepatocyte growth factor (HGF)?
cards.back: Scatter factor. HGF is both mitogenic and motogenic in epithelial repair. [19][20]

cards.bucket: Cellular activation, signaling, and growth factors
cards.front: What is the main action of PDGF in tissue repair?
cards.back: Recruitment and proliferation of fibroblasts, smooth muscle cells, and endothelial cells. PDGF is important in repair and fibrosis. [20]

cards.bucket: Cellular activation, signaling, and growth factors
cards.front: Which major proliferative pathways are activated downstream of RAS?
cards.back: MAPK and PI3K/Akt/mTOR. These are the major proliferative pathways downstream of RAS. [17]

cards.bucket: Extracellular matrix
cards.front: Which extracellular matrix structure lies beneath epithelium, endothelium, and smooth muscle?
cards.back: Basement membrane. It is the organized ECM layer beneath those cell types. [22]

cards.bucket: Extracellular matrix
cards.front: What is the major adhesive glycoprotein of basement membrane?
cards.back: Laminin. It is the dominant adhesive glycoprotein of basement membrane. [24]

cards.bucket: Extracellular matrix
cards.front: What is the main collagenous scaffold of basement membrane?
cards.back: Type IV collagen. It is the characteristic collagen of basement membrane. [22]

cards.bucket: Extracellular matrix
cards.front: What major role does fibronectin play in wound healing?
cards.back: It provides a scaffold for ECM deposition and re-epithelialization. That is its key role in wound healing. [24]

cards.bucket: Extracellular matrix
cards.front: Defect in which extracellular matrix component causes Marfan syndrome?
cards.back: Fibrillin. Defect of fibrillin causes Marfan syndrome. [23]

cards.bucket: Extracellular matrix
cards.front: What peptide motif is recognized by many integrins?
cards.back: RGD. Many integrins recognize this peptide motif on ECM proteins such as fibronectin. [24]

cards.bucket: Extracellular matrix
cards.front: What do integrins physically link to the extracellular matrix?
cards.back: The intracellular cytoskeleton. Integrins connect ECM outside the cell to cytoskeletal structures inside the cell. [24]

cards.bucket: Extracellular matrix
cards.front: What is the main function of proteoglycans in extracellular matrix?
cards.back: They create a hydrated gel and reservoir for growth factors. [23]

cards.bucket: Maintaining cell populations and regenerative medicine
cards.front: What is the restriction point of the cell cycle?
cards.back: The late G1 commitment point. After the restriction point, the cell no longer needs ongoing growth factor stimulation to continue the cycle. [24][25]

cards.bucket: Maintaining cell populations and regenerative medicine
cards.front: In which phase of the cell cycle does DNA synthesis occur?
cards.back: S phase. That is the phase of DNA synthesis. [25]

cards.bucket: Maintaining cell populations and regenerative medicine
cards.front: What drives the G1 to S transition?
cards.back: Cyclin D-CDK4/6 and cyclin E-CDK2 phosphorylating Rb. This drives the G1 to S transition. [25][26]

cards.bucket: Maintaining cell populations and regenerative medicine
cards.front: Which complex is essential for the G2 to M transition?
cards.back: Cyclin B-CDK1. It is essential for the G2 to M transition. [25][26]

cards.bucket: Maintaining cell populations and regenerative medicine
cards.front: What do p21, p27, and p57 do?
cards.back: They inhibit multiple CDKs. The Cip/Kip family restrains cell-cycle progression. [25][26]

cards.bucket: Maintaining cell populations and regenerative medicine
cards.front: What are the two defining properties of stem cells?
cards.back: Self-renewal and generation of differentiated progeny. These two features define a stem cell. [26]

cards.bucket: Maintaining cell populations and regenerative medicine
cards.front: What do adult stem-cell niches mainly do?
cards.back: They keep stem cells quiescent and regulate activation when expansion is needed. [27][28]

cards.bucket: Maintaining cell populations and regenerative medicine
cards.front: What is an induced pluripotent stem cell?
cards.back: A reprogrammed somatic cell with embryonic stem cell-like pluripotency. [28]
